One side note: SCORM is actually worth learning if you plan to pursue more corporate L&D roles — it's just the packaging format that makes Storyline courses work inside an LMS. Articulate has free tutorials and it only takes a few hours to understand the basics. Something to add to your list!

Code · HTML 

WCAG Accessibility qhat is this

6:12 PM

WCAG stands for Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — it's a set of international standards for making digital content accessible to people with disabilities (screen readers, color contrast, keyboard navigation, etc.).

In instructional design it means making sure your courses work for learners with visual, hearing, or cognitive impairments.

Do you apply accessibility practices when you design courses? For example:

  • Adding captions/subtitles to videos
  • Using sufficient color contrast
  • Writing alt text for images
  • Making sure courses can be navigated without a mouse

If you do some of these things naturally but just didn't know the term WCAG — you can keep it. If you've never thought about accessibility in your course design, let's remove it to stay honest.

Q: What should we do with WCAG Accessibility on your portfolio? A: Keep it — I do apply accessibility practices

7:00 PM

Great question! Here are your best options, from easiest to most professional:

Option 1 — GitHub Pages (free, most common for portfolios)

  1. Create a free account at github.com
  2. Create a new repository named username.github.io
  3. Upload your migdalia-portfolio.html and rename it index.html
  4. Your site goes live at https://username.github.io automatically

Option 2 — Netlify (free, easiest, no coding)

  1. Go to netlify.com and create a free account
  2. Drag and drop your HTML file onto their dashboard
  3. You get a live link instantly like https://migdalia-portfolio.netlify.app
  4. You can customize the URL for free

Option 3 — Google Sites (simplest but limited)

  • Free, no account setup beyond Gmail
  • Less flexible but gets the job done

My recommendation: Netlify. It takes literally 2 minutes — you just drag and drop the file. No coding, no setup. You get a real URL you can put on your resume and email to employers.

One important thing first — do you want a custom domain name like migdaliaperezdesigns.com? Those cost about $10-15/year on Namecheap or Google Domains and look much more professional than a .netlify.app or .github.io URL on a resume.

Which option sounds best to you? I can walk you through it step by step! 🚀

i have a github account

7:02 PM

Perfect! Here's exactly what to do step by step:

Step 1 — Download the portfolio file Click the download button on the file I gave you and save it to your computer.

Step 2 — Rename the file Rename it from migdalia-portfolio.html to index.html — this is important, GitHub won't know it's your homepage otherwise.

Step 3 — Create a new repository

  1. Go to github.com and sign in
  2. Click the + button (top right) → New repository
  3. Name it exactly: yourusername.github.io (replace with your actual GitHub username)
  4. Make sure it's set to Public
  5. Check the box "Add a README file"
  6. Click Create repository

Step 4 — Upload your file

  1. Inside the new repository, click Add file → Upload files
  2. Drag your index.html file in
  3. Scroll down and click Commit changes

Step 5 — Enable GitHub Pages

  1. Click Settings (inside the repository)
  2. Scroll down to Pages in the left menu
  3. Under "Branch" select main → click Save

Step 6 — Wait 2-3 minutes Your site will be live at: https://yourusername.github.io

What's your GitHub username? I can tell you exactly what your URL will be so you have it ready to put on your resume! 🚀

hpw the fuck do i transfer this to github everythime i hit dlaunlow it says open file and it dose not let me save it

7:32 PM

I totally understand the frustration! The issue is that your browser is opening the HTML file instead of saving it. Here's the fix:

On Windows:

  • Right-click the download link
  • Select "Save link as" (or "Save as")
  • Choose where to save it on your computer
  • Make sure the filename ends in .html

On Mac:

  • Right-click (or Control+click) the download link
  • Select "Download Linked File As"
  • Save it to your Desktop

OR — the easiest method:

  1. When the file opens in your browser
  2. Press Ctrl+S (Windows) or Cmd+S (Mac)
  3. It will save the full HTML file to your computer

Then once you have the file saved:

  1. Rename it to index.html
  2. Go to your GitHub repository
  3. Click Add file → Upload files
  4. Drag the index.html file in
  5. Click Commit changes

Which operating system are you on — Windows or Mac? I can give you more specific steps! 🎯
